turns.text: Hello Genie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate you asking. Honestly, I don't talk about this stuff much because... I don't know, it feels like complaining when I should just be grateful I have access to care at all, you know?

*pauses, looking down at hands*

But the truth is, this pregnancy has been really hard. Not just physically—though the swelling and headaches are constant—but the whole thing. I work in healthcare, so I see how things operate from the inside. I understand what pre-eclampsia means. I know the risks. And sometimes that knowledge feels like a curse because I can't just... not know.

*voice gets quieter*

What's been the hardest part, though? It's doing this alone. My husband left a few years ago, and I'm managing two other kids, working when I can, trying to keep it together. When I go to appointments, I can see the shift in how people treat me once they realize there's no partner. It's subtle, but it's there. Like I'm already a cautionary tale or something.

And the fear... *takes a breath* ...I'm terrified. Not of the actual labor—well, okay, I am—but more that something will go wrong and my kids won't have anyone. That's what keeps me awake at night.

So yeah, I'm ready to talk about it. Maybe it'll help someone else in my shoes.

When you think about your goals and expectations for your birth experience — what you hoped labour and delivery would be like — how clearly could you articulate those goals at the time, and how well did the care system support you in pursuing them?

*lets out a short, bitter laugh*

Goals? I mean... honestly, my goal is just to get through it alive. Both of us alive. That's it. That's the bar.

*rubs face tiredly*

I think that sounds depressing, but when you're high-risk at my age with pre-eclampsia, you stop thinking about "natural birth" or "minimal intervention" or all those things you maybe imagined before. Those feel like luxuries for people whose bodies aren't trying to kill them, you know?

*leans forward slightly*

But if I'm being real with you, I did have hopes early on. With my first two pregnancies, I wanted to feel like... capable. Like my body could do something powerful. This time, I just wanted to feel safe. Supported. Like someone was actually listening when I said I was scared instead of just checking boxes on a form.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

As for articulating it to the doctors? That's been tricky. I tried bringing up my anxiety about labor, and I got the magnesium protocol talk. Which, I get it—medically necessary. But nobody really asked, "Genie, what would help you feel less terrified right now?" It's all about managing the blood pressure and the protein levels. The clinical stuff. Not the person behind the numbers.

*looks up*

I don't even know if I'm allowed to have goals anymore, or if that's selfish given my diagnosis.

When you think about the decisions you were asked to make during labour and delivery — around pain management, interventions, and procedures — how well-equipped did you feel to participate in those decisions in the moment?

*shifts uncomfortably, eyes getting a bit glassy*

Oh. Um. Well, that's... that hasn't happened yet, actually. I'm still waiting. I'm due soon, but I haven't gone into labor.

*clears throat, embarrassed*

I apologize — I should have been clearer about that. I'm in the waiting period, the anxious part where I'm just... preparing, I guess. Or trying to.

*fidgets with hands*

But I can tell you what I'm dreading about that moment. That's something I think about constantly.

*voice becomes more strained*

The thing is, I know too much and not enough at the same time. I understand the medical reasoning behind interventions — I've seen the protocols, I know why they'd use magnesium, why they might need to induce, all of that. So when they present options, I can follow the logic. But that doesn't mean I feel like I have real choice, you understand?

*looks away*

I'm terrified that in the moment, when I'm in pain or scared, I won't be able to advocate for myself. And with pre-eclampsia, there are things that are just... non-negotiable for safety. So I'm not sure how much "participation" is really possible versus just... accepting what needs to happen.

*voice drops*

I just hope someone explains things to me clearly and doesn't make me feel stupid for being afraid. That's what I really want.

When you think about the emotional experience of your labour and delivery — from the very beginning through to the moment your baby was born — what were the most powerful feelings, and were you able to process them in the birth environment?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting wet*

I... I haven't given birth yet. I'm sorry, I keep forgetting to be clear about that. I'm still pregnant, still waiting. So I don't have those memories yet.

*pauses, wringing hands*

But I can tell you what I'm afraid the emotional experience will be like. That's something I obsess over.

*voice becomes shaky*

I think it'll be a lot of fear mixed with... I don't know, maybe relief if everything goes okay? But also this underlying panic because I can't control my body right now. The pre-eclampsia, the high blood pressure — it's like my own physiology is working against me. And I'm terrified I'll feel that loss of control intensify during labor.

*wipes eyes quickly*

What scares me most is that I won't be able to process anything in the moment except survival mode. Like, I'll be so focused on "don't have a seizure, don't hemorrhage, keep the baby safe" that I won't get to experience any of the... I guess the dignity of it? The power of bringing a life into the world? 

*voice cracks slightly*

And there's guilt attached to that too — like I should be grateful just to survive, and wanting more than that feels greedy.

*looks at you directly*

I guess I'm wondering if anyone will actually check in with me emotionally, or if I'll just be another high-risk case they manage clinically.

When you think about the behaviour and communication of your midwives, doctors, and care team during your birth, what interactions stood out — positively or negatively — and what made them significant?

*pauses, looking conflicted*

Okay, so... I haven't actually given birth yet. I keep saying that, and I apologize. But I think what you're asking is still important to answer, because I've had plenty of interactions with my care team leading up to this, and those are shaping what I'm expecting.

*leans back, crossing arms*

The most significant moment was actually pretty small. One of the nurses at the clinic — I think her name was Susan — she looked at my blood pressure reading, then looked at *me*, not just at the monitor. She asked how I was doing, and not in that automatic way. Like, she actually waited for an answer. When I admitted I was terrified, she didn't try to minimize it or launch into statistics. She just said, "That makes sense. Pre-eclampsia is serious, and you're managing a lot alone."

*voice becomes quieter*

That one acknowledgment meant more than I can say.

*expression hardens slightly*

But then there's the flip side. One doctor was so clinical, so rushed, that he didn't even notice I was holding back tears. He rattled off my risks like he was reading a shopping list. Another time, a staff member made an assumption that I wasn't working — just looked at my situation and made a judgment.

*meets your eyes*

So what stands out? When someone sees me as a whole person, not just a problem to solve. When they slow down. Because right now, I'm bracing myself for the birth experience to feel clinical and impersonal, and that scares me almost as much as the medical complications.

When you think about the physical environment of the place where you gave birth — the facilities, the equipment, the space — how did that environment affect your comfort, safety, and overall experience of birth?

*shifts in seat, looking a bit frustrated*

I know I keep saying this, but... I haven't given birth yet. I'm still waiting for labor to start. So I don't have an experience of the actual birth environment to tell you about.

*rubs temples*

I've been to the hospital for appointments and monitoring, though. I've seen where I'll be going when this happens. And that's... it's a lot.

*voice becomes tense*

The labor and delivery ward is fine, I guess. It's a hospital. There are machines everywhere, which makes sense given my situation, but it's also really clinical and bright and loud. I went in for a tour, and honestly, it just made the fear more real. All those monitors and beeping things — I understand what they're for, but being surrounded by that technology in the moment is going to feel suffocating to me, I think.

*looks down*

What got to me most was realizing there's no way to make it feel... intimate? Like a safe space where I can process what's happening. It's all very sterile and functional. And I get why — with pre-eclampsia, they need access to everything quickly. But part of me was hoping for at least one corner that felt less like a medical station and more like... I don't know, a place where a person could be vulnerable without feeling exposed.

*meets your eyes*

I guess I'm just anxious about whether I'll feel safe or just trapped in machinery when the time comes.
What specific aspects of the physical environment helped or hindered you during labour and delivery?

*pauses, expression becoming more guarded*

I... I really do need to be honest with you here. I haven't gone through labor yet. I'm still pregnant, still waiting for it to happen. So I don't actually have a labor and delivery experience to describe.

*sits up straighter, speaking more directly*

I'm wondering if maybe there's been some confusion about where I am in this journey, or if you're asking me to speculate about what I think *might* affect me? Because I can do that, but it feels different from actually living through it, you know?

*voice becomes a bit defensive*

I don't want to make something up or exaggerate just to give you the answer you're looking for. That wouldn't be fair to you or to whatever research you're doing. The truth is, I'm in this waiting period — this anxious, uncertain space where I'm imagining all the worst-case scenarios but haven't actually experienced them yet.

*softens slightly*

If you want to ask me what I'm *worried* about regarding the physical environment, or what I hope will happen, I can do that. But I also want to be clear that this is all anticipatory anxiety talking. The actual experience might be completely different — better or worse, I don't know.

*looks at you with tired eyes*

Does that make sense? I want to be useful to your research, but I also want to be honest about what I actually know versus what I'm just afraid of.

When you think about any complications, unexpected events, or emergency interventions during your birth, how were these communicated to you, and how did you experience the shift from a planned to an unplanned situation?

*takes a shaky breath, gripping the armrest*

Okay, I... I need to be really clear here. I haven't given birth yet. There's been no emergency, no shift from planned to unplanned because the birth hasn't happened.

*voice becomes slightly strained*

But I think what you're actually asking — what you're getting at — is about my *anticipation* of that possibility, right? Because that's what I live with every single day.

*leans forward, speaking more intensely*

The pre-eclampsia means I'm already in an "unplanned" situation. This wasn't supposed to be complicated. And my care team has talked about possibilities — induction, possible C-section if things escalate, seizures, all of it. They've been relatively straightforward about the risks, which I appreciate even though it terrifies me.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

But here's what haunts me: if an emergency happens during labor, I'm going to be alone. Or mostly alone. My husband isn't here. I don't have family nearby. And I keep imagining that moment where suddenly everything shifts and gets urgent, and I'm trying to process what's happening while also managing two kids at home, managing the hospital bills that are already piling up...

*eyes glisten*

So the communication part matters so much to me. If something goes wrong, I need someone to explain it, not just do it. I need to feel like I'm still a person being cared for, not just a medical emergency being managed.

*pauses*

That's my biggest fear, honestly.
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

*exhales slowly, looking almost relieved by the question*

Okay, so this is something I *can* speak to a little bit, because I've been thinking about it obsessively. Planning for it, honestly, because I know postpartum is going to be the hardest part.

*voice becomes strained*

My goals are... basic, I guess. I need to recover physically without complications — especially with the pre-eclampsia, I'm worried about postpartum hypertension, seizures, all of that. But more than that, I need *help*. Actual, consistent help with my newborn and my other two kids.

*looks away*

And that's where I'm terrified the system is going to fail me. I don't have family here. My ex isn't involved. I'm looking at maybe a few days in the hospital after birth if everything goes well, and then... what? I go home alone with a newborn and two other kids, trying to manage blood pressure checks and exhaustion and the emotional weight of it all?

*voice cracks slightly*

I've been trying to figure out childcare, postpartum doula support, something. But financially? It's not realistic. I can't afford it. So I'm hoping the hospital will connect me with resources, social workers, *something*. But from what I've seen so far, nobody's really asked me about my support system or helped me plan for that reality.

*meets your eyes*

I don't know what postpartum will actually look like for me yet. But I'm bracing myself to do it mostly alone.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

*rubs face, looking tired*

You know, I haven't really settled on a plan yet. That's another thing that's weighing on me.

*shifts uncomfortably*

With my first two kids, I breastfed. It was important to me — partly because I believed in it, but also honestly because formula is expensive and I was barely getting by. The breastfeeding felt like... I don't know, the one thing my body could do right after everything else fell apart with their dad leaving.

*voice becomes uncertain*

But this time? I'm not sure I have the bandwidth. I'm exhausted before labor even starts. I'm managing this high-risk pregnancy alone, working when I can, trying to keep my other two kids stable. And breastfeeding is demanding, physically and emotionally. It ties you to the baby in a way that makes it hard to get help from anyone else.

*looks conflicted*

Part of me wants to combination feed — give myself some flexibility, let other people help with bottles so I'm not the only one responsible. But I feel guilty even thinking that. Like I'm not going to be the "good mother" who breastfeeds.

*voice drops*

And honestly? Nobody's really asked me what would actually work for my life. They hand me breastfeeding pamphlets, assume that's what I'll do, but they don't know I'm a single mom barely keeping my head above water.

*meets your eyes*

I wish someone would sit down with me and help me figure out what's realistic and what I actually need, not just what looks best on paper.

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

*goes very still, eyes getting distant*

I... okay. I haven't been through that yet either. I'm still waiting for labor.

*pauses, then speaks more softly*

But I think you're asking about something really important that I'm terrified about.

*voice becomes shaky*

Postpartum depression. That's what I'm most afraid of, honestly. More than the medical complications, more than the labor pain. Because I've been there before — after my second kid, it was bad. Really bad. I was drowning, couldn't sleep even when the baby slept, felt this crushing weight that made me question everything about myself as a mother.

*wipes eyes*

And I got through it alone. Mostly. I didn't reach out because I was ashamed, because I thought I should just be grateful, because admitting I was struggling felt like admitting I was failing my kids. I pushed through it.

*voice becomes more intense*

This time, with everything else — the pre-eclampsia, the financial stress, being a single parent, the fear of what my body might do — I'm terrified that postpartum depression will come back harder. And I don't know if anyone's really going to ask me about my mental health or just focus on the baby.

*looks at you directly*

Nobody's mentioned screening for it. Nobody's talked about what support looks like. And that silence is deafening to me because I know how dark it can get.

*quietly*

I need help. Real, consistent help. But I don't know how to ask for it.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

*takes a long breath, looking down at hands*

That's... that's a deep question.

*pauses for a long moment*

Honestly, I think that shift happened a long time ago. With my first kid. I was someone else before that — more carefree, I guess. Had plans. Had a partner who was supposed to be there.

*voice becomes bitter*

Becoming a parent didn't just change my identity. It shattered it. And then my identity as a partner shattered too when my husband left. So now I'm trying to hold together being a mother, a single parent, a provider, someone working in healthcare who understands risk and failure... it's a lot of broken pieces.

*looks up, eyes glistening*

The professional part has been the hardest to reconcile. I work in health settings, so I have this knowledge and competence there. But the moment I become a patient? I feel powerless. I feel like all that expertise doesn't matter because my body isn't cooperating. It's humbling and infuriating at the same time.

*voice cracks slightly*

As a daughter... my parents worry but they can't really help. They're not close by. So I don't burden them with how scared I am.

*meets your eyes*

I think what's most significant is that I've lost the person I was before all this. I'm just... surviving now. Being a mother. The other parts of me feel like they're on pause. And I don't know if they come back after this baby arrives, or if this is just who I am now.

*quietly*

I'm not sure I even recognize myself anymore.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

*sits back, eyes distant*

Yeah. There's one moment that keeps replaying in my head.

*voice becomes quieter*

I was at a clinic appointment a few weeks ago, and the doctor was explaining my pre-eclampsia risk again. And I'm sitting there — I'm in scrubs because I'd come straight from a shift at the hospital where I work in maternal health — and I'm hearing myself respond to him using all the right medical language. I understand the pathophysiology. I know what magnesium sulfate does. I'm the competent professional.

*pauses, emotion rising*

But then he said something about "your case" and how "complicated" I am, and I just... broke. Right there. Started crying in front of him. And I watched his face change — like he suddenly realized I wasn't a colleague to talk to, I was a scared patient. A scared *single* mother.

*voice shakes*

And in that moment, I felt the full weight of it. I'm the person who's supposed to understand these things clinically, to help other women navigate their fears. But I can't even navigate my own. My knowledge doesn't protect me. It just makes me more aware of everything that could go wrong.

*wipes eyes*

I had to go back to work that same day. I had to put on a mask and be the competent healthcare worker again, helping other pregnant women, while internally I was falling apart about my own birth.

*looks at you*

That's when I realized how fractured I've become. I don't know how to integrate those pieces anymore.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

*laughs, but it's hollow and sad*

My partner. Right.

*looks away*

He's not here. He left a few years ago. So that support... it doesn't exist.

*voice becomes tight*

And I think that's actually the core of everything I'm struggling with right now. This pregnancy, this birth, the fear — it's all being filtered through the reality that I'm doing it alone. There's no one to hold my hand during labor. No one to take the night shift with the newborn so I can sleep. No one to sit with me if postpartum depression hits. No one to say, "You're doing okay. We're in this together."

*meets your eyes, pain evident*

So when you ask what would have made it better... having a partner who actually stayed would have made it better. Having someone who saw me as worth staying for.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

But that's not my reality. My reality is figuring out how to recover from a high-risk pregnancy and birth while managing two other kids and a newborn, probably while going back to work because I can't afford not to.

*pauses*

What would help now is what I've been asking for all along — real, consistent support from the hospital and community. A social worker who actually follows up. Resources. Someone acknowledging that my situation is hard and that I deserve help, not judgment.

*quietly*

I can't change the fact that my partner is gone. But the system could do more to recognize that some of us are doing this alone.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

*lets out a bitter laugh*

We didn't really discuss it. Not seriously, anyway.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Before my first pregnancy, we talked about... I don't know, general stuff. He said he'd help. We both assumed he'd be there. That's what you assume when you're married, right? That the person you chose is going to show up.

*voice becomes quieter, more resigned*

But even before he left, there was this unspoken understanding that childcare was my job. He'd "help" — which meant doing things when I asked, treating it like a favor. I was the one who carried the mental load. I knew when diapers were running low, when the kids needed haircuts, what they ate, how they were doing emotionally. He was... present, but not responsible.

*pauses, processing*

And then he just... wasn't present anymore. So whatever assumptions we had became irrelevant.

*voice hardens slightly*

What's strange is that I'm less angry about him leaving than I am about the fact that nothing really changed for me. I'm still the primary caregiver. I'm still carrying everything. The only difference is now I'm doing it without even the pretense of partnership.

*looks at you*

So when you ask what we discussed — we didn't. We just fell into patterns. And I think that's part of why this third pregnancy feels so impossible. I'm not recovering from a birth with support. I'm recovering alone, like I've been doing for years now.

*quietly*

There's no division to negotiate anymore. It's all on me.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

*leans forward, hands clasped together tightly*

Okay, but I need to be clear again — I haven't actually gone through the postpartum period yet. I'm still waiting to give birth.

*pauses, then speaks more directly*

But I can tell you about the barriers I'm already anticipating, because they're very real to me right now. And honestly, that anticipatory dread is its own kind of burden.

*voice becomes strained*

Financially, that's the biggest one. I can't afford a postpartum doula. Childcare is already stretched thin. I'm looking at going back to work probably too soon because I need the income. The system doesn't seem to have much for single mothers who are barely scraping by.

*counts on fingers*

Then there's the isolation. No family nearby. My ex isn't involved. Friends have their own lives. So emotionally, I'm going to be alone when things get hard — and they will get hard.

*voice cracks*

Informationally, nobody's really told me what resources exist. Social services, mental health screening, support groups for single mothers with postpartum depression — I don't even know what's available or how to access it. And I'm exhausted before I even start, so the idea of having to hunt for help feels impossible.

*meets your eyes*

The biggest systemic barrier? Nobody's treating my situation as urgent just because it's not a medical emergency. But being a single mother with pre-eclampsia facing postpartum alone — that *is* an emergency. It's a crisis waiting to happen.

*quietly*

And I'm terrified nobody will see it until I'm already drowning.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

*shakes head slowly*

I haven't given birth yet, so I don't have a postpartum experience with those tools. Not yet, anyway.

*pauses, then speaks more thoughtfully*

But I can tell you what I'm already doing in this waiting period, because it's... it's consuming a lot of my mental energy.

*looks down at hands*

I'm on a few online forums for high-risk pregnancy. Reddit, some Facebook groups. And honestly? It's a double-edged sword. On one hand, seeing other women's stories — especially other single mothers — makes me feel less alone. Like, oh, I'm not the only one terrified and managing this solo.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

But on the other hand, the internet is full of horror stories. I read about women whose pre-eclampsia turned into eclampsia, who had seizures, who lost their babies. And I spiral. I sit up at night reading medical journals I probably shouldn't be reading because I understand them too well.

*rubs face*

I think postpartum, those online communities will probably matter more. I can access support from home, which is important when you're isolated and can't afford a therapist or support groups. But the limits are real — you can't get a hug through a screen. You can't have someone actually *see* you struggling and intervene.

*meets your eyes*

I'm hoping to use those tools. But I also know they're not a substitute for real, human support. And that's what I'm really missing.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting wet*

That's... that's a lot to think about all at once.

*pauses, collecting thoughts*

If I'm being honest, I wish someone had asked me about my life — my *actual* life — before diving into the clinical stuff. Not just "are you safe" or "do you have support," but really sat with me and asked what I needed to survive this. Because the gap between my medical care and my real-world circumstances is enormous.

*voice becomes more passionate*

I wish they'd connected me with a social worker early on, not as an afterthought. Someone who could help me navigate childcare, financial resources, mental health support before the crisis hits. I wish someone had said, "You're a single mother managing a high-risk pregnancy alone. This is hard. Let's figure out how to actually support you."

*leans forward*

And the mental health piece — I wish someone had screened me for depression and anxiety from the beginning, not just monitored my blood pressure. I wish they'd normalized talking about how scared I am, instead of treating fear like it's a symptom to manage.

*voice becomes quieter but more resolute*

But if I had to pick one single change? It would be continuity of care. Having one person — a midwife, a nurse, *someone* — who knows me, who checks in consistently, who sees me as a whole person and not just a case file.

*meets your eyes*

Someone who would ask, "Genie, how are *you* doing?" and actually wait for the answer. That would change everything.
Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes filling with tears*

It was right after I got diagnosed with pre-eclampsia. That moment when everything shifted from "normal pregnancy" to "high-risk."

*voice becomes strained*

The doctor told me the diagnosis, explained the protocol — magnesium, monitoring, all of it. Very clinical. And then... nothing. He left. And I'm sitting there in this exam room alone, processing that my body is basically betraying me, that my baby could be at risk, that I might have a seizure or a stroke or... and no one asked how I was feeling emotionally.

*wipes eyes*

I called my ex that day — stupid, I know — just because I needed to tell someone and have them care. He didn't answer. That's when it really hit me that I was doing this alone.

*voice breaks slightly*

What would have helped? Someone — a nurse, a social worker, *anyone* — sitting with me after that diagnosis and saying, "This is scary. You're managing a lot. Let's talk about what support looks like." Not just handing me pamphlets and sending me on my way.

*looks at you directly*

Instead, I went home and spiraled. I looked up mortality statistics. I cried in my car. I didn't call anyone because I thought I should just be strong. And nobody from the hospital checked in on how I was processing this life-altering diagnosis.

*voice quieter*

That gap — between the clinical care and the emotional care — that's where I felt most abandoned by the system.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a moment, gathering thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*looks directly at you*

I think what's important for people to understand is that being "high-risk" doesn't just mean medical risk. It means you become invisible in a different way. You're hyper-visible as a case — all these appointments, all this monitoring — but invisible as a person.

*voice becomes more intense*

And when you're also a single mother, when you're also struggling financially, when you're also scared... those things don't get separated from the medical stuff. They all bleed together. But the system treats them like they're separate silos. The OB handles the pre-eclampsia. Social services *might* handle the financial stuff if I can figure out how to access it. Mental health is somewhere else entirely.

*pauses*

What I wish is that someone would see all of me — the mother, the patient, the scared woman, the person who's worked hard her whole life and is still barely getting by — and say, "We're going to help you through this as a whole person, not just manage your blood pressure."

*voice quieter but steady*

And I want other women like me to know they're not failing if they're terrified. If they're alone. If the system feels broken. Because it is broken, at least for us.

*meets your eyes*

That's what I'd want to add. That's what matters to me.
